one_line_takeaway: Combining LLM semantic judgment with cosine-similarity scoring lets a knowledge-based authenticator accept 99.5% of paraphrased-but-correct answers at a 0.1% false-acceptance rate, and grounding an LLM scam detector with RAG cuts its false-positive rate from 17.2% to 3.5% on the authors' own datasets.
key_finding: Authentication: with Llama-3.3, the hybrid (LLM + cosine-similarity) evaluator accepts 99.5% of legitimate non-exact (paraphrased) answers while holding the false-acceptance rate of incorrect answers to 0.1%; at a cosine-similarity threshold of 1.0 (exact match required) false-acceptance is 0, and false acceptance rises as the threshold is relaxed below 1.0, per Figures 5-6 (threshold range 0.7-1.0). Both ChatGPT-4 and Llama-3.3 show a clear positional bias, generating disproportionately more security questions from the beginning and end segments of a document than from the middle segments (Figure 2, averaged over 3 documents x 20 questions each). Fraud detection: ChatGPT-4 without RAG has a false-negative/recall-miss rate of 7.30% and a false-positive rate of 17.20%; with RAG enabled, false-negative rate drops to 5.80% and false-positive rate drops to 3.50% (Table 2).
core_contribution: The paper (Emunah S. S. Chan and Aldar C-F. Chan) presents two separate LLM-enabled systems, both original to this work: (1) an LLM-assisted user-authentication mechanism that replaces exact-match knowledge-based security questions with an LLM-driven semantic evaluation of natural-language answers, combined with two specific novel mitigations -- document segmentation (to counter LLMs' observed positional bias toward generating questions from the beginning/end of a source document) and a hybrid scoring method that fuses LLM judgment with cosine-similarity between sentence-embedding vectors of the user's and reference answers; and (2) a RAG-based fraud/scam-detection pipeline in which an LLM extracts message features (intent, tone, urgency, requested actions, entities), retrieves supporting evidence from curated repositories (confirmed scam databases, organizational policy documents), and produces a grounded scam-likelihood score plus explanation, intended to reduce hallucination and false positives and to adapt to new scam tactics by updating the retrieval corpus rather than retraining the model.
threat_model: For the authentication component: an adversary who tries to defeat security-question-based authentication, either by exploiting rigid exact-match systems (locking out legitimate users) or by socially engineering/publicly discovering answers to fixed questions; the paper also treats an adversary who could exploit positional bias in LLM-generated questions by concentrating information-gathering on the start of a target document. For the fraud-detection component: fraudsters (phishing, romance scams, business email compromise, scam messages) whose evolving tactics defeat static/classifier-based detectors; the defender's goal is to correctly flag fraudulent messages while minimizing false positives that misclassify legitimate messages as scams.
limitations: The paper explicitly states its own limitation: further experiments on new, evolving-scam-tactic datasets are needed to fully evaluate the RAG system's adaptability, but scarcity of publicly available up-to-date real-world fraud pattern data makes constructing such datasets difficult. Beyond the paper's stated limitation, the described methodology also relies on a self-constructed 1,000-item authentication Q&A dataset and a synthetic scam-message dataset (built atop an existing public synthetic dataset), rather than data drawn from real deployed systems or real fraud victims; the fraud-detection results are explicitly labeled 'preliminary'; only one LLM per experiment track (Llama-3.3 for authentication threshold sweep, ChatGPT-4 for fraud detection) is used for the headline quantitative results, with the positional-bias check being the only place both models are compared directly.

datasets_used: Self-constructed dataset of 1,000 security questions and model answers (Appendix A; released at huggingface.co/datasets/emunah/authentication)
Three documents used for the positional-bias question-generation experiment (segmented into 4 equal word-count parts each)
Synthetic scam-message dataset built from Gumphusiri's 'Synthetic Data for Scam Detection' (ref [9], huggingface.co/BothBosu), combined with a set of legitimate messages

RAG retrieval corpus: a labeled set of confirmed scam messages with red-flag attributes (fake links, account-lock threats, urgent language), generated/labeled by a separate ChatGPT-4 instance (Appendix B)
quantitative_results: Authentication (Llama-3.3-70B): 99.5% acceptance of legitimate non-exact answers, 0.1% false-acceptance rate of incorrect answers

Authentication: false-acceptance rate = 0 at cosine-similarity threshold = 1.0 (exact match), rising as threshold decreases toward 0.7 (exact values plotted in Figures 5-6, not tabulated numerically in extracted text)

Positional bias: ChatGPT-4 and Llama-3.3 both generate a disproportionate share of the 20 questions/document from segment 1 and segment 4 versus segments 2-3, averaged over 3 documents (exact per-segment percentages given only in Figure 2, not as text/table)
Fraud detection (ChatGPT-4) without RAG: false-negative/recall-miss rate 7.30%, false-positive rate 17.20%
Fraud detection (ChatGPT-4) with RAG: false-negative/recall-miss rate 5.80%, false-positive rate 3.50%
baselines_compared: Exact-match / traditional knowledge-based authentication (security questions requiring exact string matches) as the implicit baseline for the authentication system
ChatGPT-4 without RAG as the baseline for the RAG-enhanced fraud detector (Table 2)
Cosine-similarity threshold = 1.0 (exact match) used as an internal baseline point within the authentication threshold sweep
